13: The Deuteronomistic historian’s summary of the entire prophetic mission to Israel and Judah — wayyāʿaḏ YHWH bᵉyiśrāʾēl ûḇîhûḏāh bᵉyaḏ kol nᵉḇîʾê kol ḥōzeh lēʾmōr šûḇû miḏḏarḵêḵem hārāʿôt, ‘yet YHWH warned Israel and Judah by every prophet and every seer, saying: Turn from your evil ways and keep my commandments and my statutes’ — is the OT’s most comprehensive statement of the prophetic mission as warning-before-judgment. The entire prophetic corpus is here summarized as a sustained act of YHWH’s patience: every prophet sent, every seer commissioned, all bearing the same summons to return. The nation refused. Jesus’s lament over Jerusalem in Matthew 23:37 inhabits this same framework: Ierousalēm Ierousalēm hē apokteinousa tous prophētas kai lithobologousa tous apestalmenos pros autēn, posakis ēthelēsa episynagagein ta tekna sou — ‘O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets and stones those who are sent to it! How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing.’ The structure is identical: YHWH sent every prophet; Israel refused every prophet; judgment followed. Jesus as the final and definitive sender-and-sent (John 20:21: ‘as the Father has sent me, even so I am sending you’) is the culmination of the prophetic mission that 17:13 summarizes. Stephen in Acts 7:52 makes the typological sequence explicit: tina tōn prophētōn ouk ediōxan hoi pateres hymōn — ‘which of the prophets did your fathers not persecute?’ — and concludes with the betrayal and murder of ‘the Righteous One.’ The 17:13 summary is the OT’s own retrospective on why the Servant who was sent had to be rejected.
1: Hoshea’s accession — the last king of Israel. The Northern Kingdom’s final king reigns nine years (c. 732–722 BCE) before Samaria falls. The end of the Israelite monarchy in the north is the terminus of a two-century experiment in covenant-defined kingship that repeatedly failed. Christ comes as the fulfillment of everything that Israelite kingship pointed toward but never achieved: the king who perfectly serves YHWH, perfectly protects the people, and reigns forever.
2: Hoshea did evil — but not like the kings of Israel before him. The qualified condemnation is unusual; perhaps Hoshea showed some minimal improvement. But “not as evil as others” is still evil. Against a standard of perfection, partial improvement is insufficient. Christ’s righteousness is not “better than” but “complete” — “the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe” (Romans 3:22). The comparison standard is not other kings but God’s own holiness.
3: Shalmaneser (V) marches against Hoshea, who becomes his vassal. The submission to Assyrian power — paying tribute, becoming a dependent client — is the final humiliation before the deportation. What Menahem purchased (Assyrian protection for tribute), Hoshea inherits and then tries to escape (v4). The cycle of tribute-and-dependency is what Christ breaks: “For freedom Christ has set us free; stand firm therefore, and do not submit again to a yoke of slavery” (Galatians 5:1).
4: Hoshea’s conspiracy — sending envoys to Egypt (“So king of Egypt” is uncertain; perhaps Sibe/Osorkon IV) and withholding tribute. The double-dealing that gets Hoshea imprisoned is the northern equivalent of trusting in Egypt rather than YHWH (Isaiah 31:1-3). The prophet Isaiah repeatedly condemns reliance on Egypt’s military: “The Egyptians are man and not God, and their horses are flesh and not spirit” (Isaiah 31:3). Christ is the one on whom trust is rightly placed — not flesh and not spirit in that sense, but the incarnate Word who is both human and divine.
5: Three-year siege of Samaria. The prolonged siege — years of suffering before the final capitulation — is the covenant judgment for Israel’s sustained rejection of YHWH over generations. Against the patience of the siege image, Christ’s parables of patient waiting resonate: the father who ran to meet the returning son had waited long (Luke 15:20). But the siege also pictures the finality of judgment when it comes: “He who is unjust, let him be unjust still” (Revelation 22:11). The time for repentance had passed; the siege closed.
6: Samaria falls; Israel deported to Halah, Habor, and Median cities. The exile is the ultimate covenant curse — loss of land, loss of temple, loss of national identity. Yet it is also the context for some of the richest messianic prophecy: Isaiah 40-55 (written to address the exile) is saturated with prophecy of the servant who would bear Israel’s iniquities. The Babylonian exile of Judah will produce the same effect: loss becomes the womb of promise. Christ bears the ultimate exile (“My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” — Matthew 27:46) so that his people need not be permanently exiled from God.
7: The Deuteronomistic theological explanation begins: “This happened because the Israelites had sinned against the LORD their God.” The theological interpretation of historical events is the Deuteronomist’s distinctive contribution — not merely recording what happened but why. Christ’s cross is the ultimate interpretive event: “For Christ died for sins once for all, the righteous for the unrighteous, to bring you to God” (1 Peter 3:18). The “this happened because” logic of Deuteronomistic history reaches its apex at Calvary, where the “why” is the accumulated sin of all humanity.
8: They followed the practices of the nations the LORD had expelled and of their own kings. The double source of contamination — pagan nations and apostate kings — created a feedback loop of covenant violation. The law that was supposed to set Israel apart (Deuteronomy 4:6-8: “your wisdom in the sight of the peoples”) was abandoned in favor of imitation of the expelled nations. Christ restores the holy-nation identity: “You are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation” (1 Peter 2:9) — the identity Israel was called to be but failed to maintain.
9: The Israelites secretly did things not right — high places from watchtower to fortified city. The secrecy of their apostasy (“secretly” — be-seter) reveals guilt-consciousness: they knew what they were doing was wrong. This inner knowledge of wrongdoing is what Paul describes as the witness of conscience (Romans 2:15: “they show that the work of the law is written on their hearts, their conscience bearing witness”). The secret sin that conscience knows is precisely what Christ’s atonement addresses at its deepest level.
10: Sacred pillars and Asherah poles on every high hill and under every green tree. The proliferation of worship sites is the spatial expression of the broken covenant — worship scattered everywhere instead of concentrated at the divine meeting point. Christ draws all worship to himself as the single true altar and sanctuary: “When I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself” (John 12:32). The scattered-everywhere worship of the high places finds its answer in the crucified, raised Christ who becomes the universal center of true worship.
11: Incense burned at high places as the nations did — whom YHWH expelled. The worship Israel does is described as mimicking expelled nations whose worship God rejected. The irony is double: Israel worships like the nations; the nations are expelled for this worship; therefore Israel earns the same expulsion. But Christ breaks this cycle: “He has abolished the law of commandments expressed in ordinances, that he might create in himself one new man in place of the two, so making peace” (Ephesians 2:15). In Christ, Gentile and Jew together worship rightly.
12: They served idols despite the explicit divine prohibition. The willful violation of clear command — “You must not do this” — is the classic definition of sin as transgression. Christ bears the condemnation of this transgression: “He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree” (1 Peter 2:24). The idol-worship explicitly prohibited in the first commandment is precisely what required the atoning work that Christ performs at the cross.
14: They would not listen — as stubborn as their ancestors who did not trust YHWH. The ancestral pattern of hardness of heart becomes Israel’s inheritance. Jesus describes the same pattern to his generation: “You stiff-necked people, uncircumcised in heart and ears, you always resist the Holy Spirit. As your fathers did, so do you” (Acts 7:51, Stephen’s speech). The hardness of heart that led to Israel’s exile is the same hardness that leads to the rejection of Christ — and what breaks it is not better teaching but heart-surgery: “I will give you a new heart” (Ezekiel 36:26).
15: They rejected the covenant, pursued vanity (hevel), and became vain themselves — imitating the idols. The word hevel (vanity/breath) is the key term of Ecclesiastes: life oriented around things that are not God becomes as insubstantial as breath. “Those who make idols are like them; so are all who trust in them” (Psalms 115:8). Those who imitate empty things become empty; those who are united to Christ are transformed into his image: “We all, with unveiled face, beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image” (2 Corinthians 3:18).
16: They cast two metal calves, made an Asherah, worshipped the host of heaven, and served Baal. The full catalog of northern apostasy: Jeroboam’s calves, Canaanite fertility religion, astral worship, Baal. Each false object of worship is a distortion of what is rightly sought in God alone. Christ is “the image of the invisible God, the firstborn of all creation” (Colossians 1:15) — the true visible form of the God who cannot be captured in calves or pillars. Every idol is a failed attempt to have what only Christ provides: access to the living God through a visible mediator.
17: Child sacrifice, divination, sorcery — Israel gave itself to do evil. The final catalog echoes Manasseh’s crimes (2 Kings 21:6) — the north doing what the worst southern king later does. The offering of children to Molech is the demonic inversion of the gospel: false religion demands the death of children; the gospel announces that God gives his Son so that children may live. “God so loved the world that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life” (John 3:16) — the anti-Molech statement of Scripture.
18: YHWH extremely angry, expelled them from his sight; only Judah remained. The divine anger that expels — “from his sight” (me-al panav) — is the language of exile in the deepest sense: removed from divine presence. Jesus experiences this on the cross: “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?” (Matthew 27:46) — the ultimate expulsion from the divine face. Christ endures the “expelled from his sight” experience so that his people need not be permanently expelled from God’s presence.
19: Judah also did not obey — followed the practices Israel introduced. The northern infection spread south; the exile that fell on Israel will eventually fall on Judah too (586 BCE). Paul universalizes this: “Both Jews and Greeks are under sin” (Romans 3:9). The Deuteronomist shows the progressive failure of both kingdoms; Paul shows the universal condition. Christ is the answer to both: “There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus” (Romans 8:1) — the condemnation that fell on both Israel (721) and Judah (586) is absorbed by the one who was condemned so they need not be.
20: YHWH rejected all the seed of Israel, afflicted them, gave them into the hand of raiders. The rejection language — va-yim'as YHWH be-khol-zera Yisrael (the LORD rejected all the seed of Israel) — is the culmination of the covenant lawsuit. But Paul argues that even this rejection is not the final word: “Has God rejected his people? By no means!” (Romans 11:1). The exile-rejection of 2 Kings 17 generates the question of Romans 11; the answer is Christ through whom the rejected find ultimate acceptance.
21: When Israel was torn from the house of David, Jeroboam led them away from following YHWH. The division of the kingdom is traced back to Jeroboam as its agent. The split between Israel and Judah is the political expression of covenant fragmentation — one people becoming two, the northern tribe separated from the Davidic throne. Christ reunites: “For he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility” (Ephesians 2:14). The north-south division that Jeroboam institutionalized, Christ overcomes in a deeper register.
22: The Israelites continued in all of Jeroboam’s sins — never turned away from them. The power of entrenched generational sin — never turning from it — is the exact diagnosis that only the new covenant addresses. Jeremiah 31:34: “No longer shall each one teach his neighbor and his brother, saying, ‘Know the LORD,’ for they shall all know me” — the knowledge of God that Israel pursued through covenant but never internalized becomes an accomplished reality through the Spirit Christ sends.
23: The LORD removed Israel from his sight, as declared through all his servants the prophets. So Israel was exiled from their land to Assyria. The exile fulfills the prophetic word — every prophet who warned of exactly this outcome (Amos 5:27; Hosea 9:3; Micah 1:6-9) is vindicated. The prophetic word that predicted exile is the same word that predicts restoration through the servant of Isaiah 40-55. Christ is the one through whom the prophetic word of restoration is fulfilled: “Today this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing” (Luke 4:21).
24: The Assyrian king populates Samaria with Babylonians, Cutheans, and others — the origin of the Samaritans. The mixed population brought in to replace the exiled northern Israelites becomes the Samaritans of NT times. Jesus’s encounter with the Samaritan woman (John 4) is the direct redemptive engagement with this history: the people produced by Israel’s exile and Assyrian resettlement become recipients of the gospel precisely when Jesus sits at Jacob’s well in Samaria.
25: The newcomers do not fear YHWH; lions are sent among them. The lions as divine judgment on those who do not acknowledge the god of the land resonates with the broader theme: to inhabit the land without knowing its Lord brings destruction. Christ addresses the Samaritans’ incomplete knowledge directly in John 4:22: “You worship what you do not know; we worship what we know, for salvation is from the Jews.” The ignorance that invited lions in 2 Kings 17 is the same ignorance Jesus addresses in John 4 — with revelation rather than judgment.
26: The king of Assyria is told the peoples don’t know the requirements of the god of the land. The concept of a god whose “requirements” (mishpat) must be known to avoid disaster reflects ANE territorial deity theology. But YHWH is not a territorial deity; he is the Creator of all. Christ transcends territorial religion entirely: “God so loved the world” — not Israel alone, not Samaria alone, but the cosmos. The Assyrian king’s pragmatic theology (learn the local god’s rules) is vastly less than what Christ reveals about the God who seeks worshipers “in spirit and truth.”
27: An exiled Israelite priest is sent back to Bethel to teach them. The compromise — an Israelite priest teaching pagan settlers at Bethel (the site of Jeroboam’s calves) — produces syncretism rather than genuine covenant worship (v33). The inadequacy of religious instruction without heart transformation is the lesson. Christ sends the Spirit to produce genuine transformation: “But the Helper, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, he will teach you all things” (John 14:26). External religious instruction could not create what the Spirit produces internally.
28: One priest returns to Bethel and teaches them to fear YHWH. The single returned priest teaching the Samaritans at Bethel is a pale foreshadowing of the twelve apostles sent to all nations. But the instruction produces mixed results (v33: they feared YHWH and served their own gods). Christ sends the Spirit precisely to go beyond instruction to transformation: “Not because I desired a gift, but I desire fruit that may abound to your account” (Philippians 4:17). Instruction plants seeds; the Spirit produces fruit.
29: Each national group made its own gods and placed them in the Samaritan shrines. The religious pluralism of the resettled Samaria — every group keeping its own deity while nominally acknowledging YHWH — is the syncretism that Jesus directly addresses in John 4:22. The Samaritan woman’s “you worship on this mountain” and Jesus’s response about spirit-and-truth worship cuts through the entire Samaritan-syncretic compromise. Christ replaces the many deities with one worship in one Spirit.
30: Succoth-benoth (Babylonian), Nergal (Cuthean), Ashima (Hamathite) — the pantheon of resettled Samaria. The catalog of pagan deities installed in YHWH’s land is the ultimate irony: the land promised to Abraham and conquered under Joshua now hosts Babylonian and Mesopotamian gods. Paul’s Areopagus speech addresses the Athenian version of this: “Whom therefore you ignorantly worship, him I declare to you” (Acts 17:23). The unknown/multiple gods of the nations find their answer in the one God who has made himself known in Christ.
31: The Avvites and Sepharvites worship Nibhaz, Tartak, Adrammelech, and Anammelech — including child sacrifice to the last two. The catalog of gods requiring child sacrifice appears on both sides of the exile: before (Manasseh’s Molech worship in Judah, 2 Kings 21:6) and after (Sepharvite worship in resettled Samaria). The anti-type is Christ’s proclamation “Let the children come to me, and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven” (Matthew 19:14) — the children that false gods demanded as victims, Christ welcomes as heirs.
32: They feared YHWH and appointed from their own ranks priests for the high places. The self-appointed priesthood — not Levitical, not according to the Torah — is the mark of the syncretistic system. Christ appoints his own priesthood not from human lineage but through the Spirit: “You are a royal priesthood” (1 Peter 2:9) — all believers constitute the priestly community, not through Levitical descent or self-appointment but through union with the great high priest.
33: They worshipped YHWH and served their own gods. The double loyalty — YHWH and the national deities simultaneously — is the exact syncretism Jesus rules out: “No one can serve two masters” (Matthew 6:24). The Samaritan compromise of v33 is the social form of the divided heart that Jesus diagnoses in the Sermon on the Mount. The exclusive claim of Christ (“I am the way, the truth, and the life; no one comes to the Father except through me” — John 14:6) ends the syncretistic option.
34: To this day they follow their former practices — they do not truly fear YHWH. The narrator’s “to this day” reflects the persistence of Samaritan syncretism into the author’s present. The encounter of Jesus with the Samaritan woman in John 4 takes place in the context of precisely this ongoing religious compromise — which is why his offer of “living water” and “worship in spirit and truth” is so radical. Christ addresses not just an individual woman but the centuries-long failure that 2 Kings 17:34 documents.
35: YHWH’s covenant command: “Do not worship other gods.” The Deuteronomic monotheism that the resettled Samaritans violated is stated in its clearest form. Jesus recapitulates and centralizes this: “Hear, O Israel: the Lord our God, the Lord is one. And you shall love the Lord your God with all your heart” (Mark 12:29-30). The Shema that stands behind v35’s prohibition is the commandment Christ calls the greatest — and which he himself perfectly embodied.
36: Fear the LORD who brought you out of Egypt — he is the one to worship. The Exodus foundation of monotheism: YHWH’s identity is defined by the great redemptive act of deliverance. Christ performs the greater Exodus (Luke 9:31 — Moses and Elijah discuss his exodon) delivering from a greater bondage than Egypt. The “bow to him and offer sacrifices to him” prescribed in v36 finds its NT form in “present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God” (Romans 12:1).
37: “Carefully observe forever the statutes and ordinances he wrote for you.” The written Torah given at Sinai is the covenant standard the Samaritans did not follow. But the new covenant addresses the insufficiency of written law alone: “I will put my law within them and write it on their hearts” (Jeremiah 31:33). Christ does not abolish the Torah (Matthew 5:17) but writes it internally through the Spirit (2 Corinthians 3:3: “written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts”).
38: “Do not forget the covenant I have made with you.” Covenant-forgetting is the root of Israel’s and Samaria’s failure. Hebrews addresses the danger of spiritual drift and forgetting to the church: “Therefore we must pay much closer attention to what we have heard, lest we drift away from it” (Hebrews 2:1). The warning against forgetting the covenant is renewed in the new covenant as a warning against neglecting so great a salvation (Hebrews 2:3).
39: “Fear only the LORD your God, and he will rescue you from all your enemies.” The divine promise conditional on exclusive covenant loyalty — rescue in exchange for undivided fear. Christ makes the rescue unconditional through his own faithfulness: “He has delivered us from the domain of darkness and transferred us to the kingdom of his beloved Son” (Colossians 1:13). The conditional rescue of the covenant (if you fear him, he will rescue) becomes unconditional rescue through the Son who is perfectly faithful on behalf of those who are not.
40: But they would not listen — they kept to their old ways. The finality of Samaritan covenant-refusal is stated simply. The old ways prove stronger than the instruction. This is precisely the point of Jeremiah’s new covenant oracle (Jeremiah 31:31-34): the old covenant failed not because it was defective but because the people could not keep it. The new covenant Christ mediates addresses the root: “I will put my law within them” — not as external instruction but as internal reality through the Spirit.
41: So these peoples feared YHWH and served their own idols — their children and grandchildren do the same today. The persistence of Samaritan syncretism across generations — children and grandchildren maintaining the compromise — is the final word of 2 Kings 17. Against this multigenerational persistence of mixed-worship, the gospel effects a permanent break: “Old things have passed away; behold, all things have become new” (2 Corinthians 5:17). The Samaritan woman at the well represents the living reality of what 2 Kings 17 documents as dead tradition — and in John 4, Christ offers the living water that ends the multigenerational compromise.
